skill.name: enact-hello-python
skill.description: Run a minimal Enact Python greeting tool that accepts a name and prints a greeting with runtime details.
skill.summary: Runs a small Python greeting example through Enact.
skill.author: EnactProtocol

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged one external command pattern and two high-severity weak-cryptography patterns. Review found the weak-cryptography alerts are false positives on description text, and the command usage is a simple local Python invocation for an example skill.

low_findings.title: External Command Template Uses Unsanitized Name Argument
low_findings.description: The skill command runs a local Python script with the provided name value. This is expected for the tool, but the template passes user input into a command string, so shell interpretation depends on the Enact runtime.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command template is directly visible, but the available files do not show how Enact escapes substituted input. The script itself only reads argv and prints output.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Shell Example
low_findings.description: Static analysis reported Ruby or shell backtick execution, but the cited lines are a fenced bash usage example. No executable code is defined on those lines.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited text is a Markdown code fence containing a sample command. It is documentation, not code executed by the skill.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptography Alert on Skill Description
low_findings.description: Static analysis reported a weak cryptographic algorithm on the skill description line. The line contains plain metadata, not hashing or encryption logic.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line is a YAML description string. There is no cryptographic API, algorithm name, or security-sensitive operation present.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptography Alert on Input Description
low_findings.description: Static analysis reported a weak cryptographic algorithm on the name field description. The line describes a user input field and does not perform cryptography.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited line is schema documentation for the name input. No hashing, encryption, or secret handling appears in context.

dangerous_patterns.title: Command String with User-Controlled Placeholder
dangerous_patterns.description: The command string includes the ${name} placeholder. This is a common tool pattern, but marketplaces should confirm the runtime escapes arguments safely.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The placeholder is visible in the command string, but the repository snippet does not include runtime substitution behavior.

content.value_statement: New Enact users need a small example that proves tool execution works. This skill runs a Python greeting command and returns simple runtime output.
actual_capabilities: Accepts a name parameter for a personalized greeting.
Runs a local Python script inside the configured Enact environment.
Prints the generated greeting to standard output.
Shows the generation timestamp from the Python runtime.
Shows the active Python version used by the script.
limitations: It only demonstrates a greeting workflow.
It does not call external services or APIs.
It does not persist files, state, or configuration.
Input handling depends on the Enact command runtime.

use_cases.title: Verify Enact Setup
use_cases.description: Run a small skill to confirm that Enact can execute Python commands locally.

use_cases.title: Teach Skill Basics
use_cases.description: Use the simple input and output shape to explain how an Enact skill is structured.

use_cases.title: Create a Starter Template
use_cases.description: Adapt the greeting script into a small custom Python command for internal examples.

prompt_templates.title: Run the Default Greeting
prompt_templates.prompt: Use enact-hello-python with the default name and show me the greeting output.

prompt_templates.title: Run a Named Greeting
prompt_templates.prompt: Run enact-hello-python with the name Alice and summarize the output.

prompt_templates.title: Explain the Skill Flow
prompt_templates.prompt: Review enact-hello-python and explain how the input schema, command, and Python script work together.

prompt_templates.title: Adapt the Example
prompt_templates.prompt: Use enact-hello-python as a starting point and propose changes for a Python skill that accepts two text inputs.

output_examples.output: A personalized greeting for Alice, followed by the generation time and Python version.
A default greeting for World, followed by runtime metadata.

best_practices: Use simple names without shell metacharacters when testing command substitution.
Treat this as a learning example before adapting it for production tasks.
Review command templates when adding new user-controlled inputs.
anti_patterns: Do not use this skill for production identity, messaging, or notification workflows.
Do not pass secrets, credentials, or sensitive personal data as the name value.
Do not assume the example validates or sanitizes all possible input strings.

faq.question: What does this skill do?
faq.answer: It runs a Python script that prints a greeting, timestamp, and Python version.

faq.question: Does it require network access?
faq.answer: No evidence of network access appears in the reviewed files.

faq.question: Can I change the greeting name?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill defines a name input with a default value of World.

faq.question: Is this intended for production use?
faq.answer: No. It is best suited as a small Enact and Python example.

faq.question: Why is the security risk low?
faq.answer: The script is simple, but the command template includes user input passed into command execution.

faq.question: Does it store any data?
faq.answer: No evidence of file storage or persistent state appears in the reviewed files.
